data.title: ANGI's Plumbing Is Fixed, Demand Could Come Back
data.description:
ANGI Inc. (ANGI) operates a digital marketplace that connects homeowners with local service professionals across home repair and improvement categories. We rate Angi a Buy, driven by a reset cost structure, improving leading operating KPIs, and a valuation that implies little credit for recent organization-wide changes and growth. After several years of prioritizing profitability over top line growth, Angi now generates sustained free cash flow and enters 2026 positioned to translate incremental revenue into attractive contribution margins.

ANGI dropped the "list" in March 2021. While the company may have saved on three consonants and a vowel in future branding, shareholders have paid a steeper price — a roughly 90% decline in market capitalization since the change. Nearly three years into a turnaround formerly overseen by IAC, the only list Angi is now trying to shake is the underperformers. The studs have been stripped, costs reset, and the foundation appears back on solid ground.

What We Are Watching

The performance across the next 12 months rests on the team’s ability to reopen the growth spigot while maintaining its commitment to operational discipline imparted since 2023. Since then, the focus has been on removing empty calories from the menu, or complex services that provided top-line growth, but little leverage as measured by the bottom line. FY 2022 marked the last year of positive year-over-year revenue growth (+9%), achieved at the expense of cash balances, which decreased by ~$100 million. Since then, management has pivoted decisively toward profitability. Free cash flow has turned sustainably positive on the back of double-digit margin expansion, enabling over $160 million in share repurchases since 2023.

At the time of this article, we are inside of 3 weeks from the 4Q25 earnings call, and one question remains paramount — is Angi finally positioned for a reset. Has the plumbing been laid for “Jobs Done Well” to spark the stock and drive a rerating? We leave crystal balls, tarot cards and clairvoyance to the professionals.

What we do know is that following 3 consecutive years of revenue decline, early leading indicators are beginning to inflect. Pro retention, proprietary service requests, and revenue per lead have all shown improvement and bode well for both top line growth and operating margins (fewer dollars spent on Pro acquisition). Management continues to guide to a return to growth in 2026, with incremental revenue expected to flow through at attractive contribution margins given the reset cost structure. However, it’s been 3 years since investors have seen revenue growth out of Angi, and the Street is justifiably incredulous.

Recent Corporate Decisions

In early January, ANGI announced an organization-wide reduction in force impacting approximately 350 employees, generating $70–$80 million in annual run-rate savings. This excludes roughly $25 million in one-time restructuring charges expected across 4Q25 and 1Q26. The impacted roles skewed heavily toward higher compensation positions, with average salaries exceeding $200 thousand annually. We expect greater clarity to be provided at the upcoming earnings call, quelling execution concerns ahead of what’s meant to be a watershed year. Hindsight is 20/20, and revisiting the 3Q25 tea leaves, we’re not surprised by the Reduction in Force. The latest shareholder letter alluded to the merging of US and International Product and Technology organizations aimed at a unified platform. This is the International playbook CEO Jeff Kip enacted across the International division that gave way to meaningful EBITDA margin expansion in those units. Now, that same focus is shifting to the US where the goal is migrating to a single global platform by 2Q27 from 4 today. Pulling on that thread, we suspect the Reductions in Force (RIFs) had an outsized impact on these departments given the implied comp p/employee and reduced need for Prod and Tech (Engineering/Infrastructure) support.

ANGI Valuation Before and After the Reduction In Force

Using pre-RIF guidance, we arrive at our 12 million price target of $20 by applying a 6x multiple to our adjusted 2026 EBITDA estimate. Using pre-RIF guidance, we arrive at a $20 price target by applying a 6x multiple to our adjusted 2026 EBITDA estimate. Conservatively capitalizing just 50% of the announced run-rate savings — approximately $37.5 million — implies incremental enterprise value of roughly $225 million at the same multiple. After adjusting for net debt and share count, this equates to approximately $6 per share of additional equity value, implying a Price Target closer to $26.

The stock reacted constructively to the right-sizing in the days that followed the release, but since the announcement, has retreated back to pre-RIF levels. To reiterate, the Street is incredulous, and as such, awarded no real lift to shares.

Proposed reasons for the underwhelming response might include 1) Are the expense saves meant to offset (or distract) a tempered 2026 revenue outlook; 2) Are the sustainability and quality of savings structural or is Management banking short-term saves at the expense of long-term underinvestment; 3) Finally, execution. We are assuming a 50% execution discount to the guided run-rate savings of $70-$80 million, said differently, our modeling assumes just $37.5 million of additional EBITDA tied to the right sizing. Given the lack of reaction in share price, the market appears even more skeptical of the team’s ability to execute the full savings in perpetuity. We cannot tell for sure, but what we are confident in is that Angi is a show-me story and investors on the sideline want actual results before stepping in.

Time will tell whether “Jobs Done Well” becomes more than a slogan. For now, ANGI remains a show-me story, but with costs reset and operating metrics inflecting favorably, ANGI’s list of required proof points is getting shorter. If Management delivers, the market won’t need much persuading to award the team for executing a multi-year turnaround.

Catalysts

A return to revenue growth in 2026, improving leading indicators (pro retention, proprietary service requests, and revenue p/lead), and incremental margin expansion from the recently reset cost structure could drive a valuation rerating.

Risks

Execution risk remains elevated, including the durability of cost savings, the ability to reaccelerate growth without sacrificing the margin gains achieved during the reset, and lingering investor skepticism after multiple years of revenue decline.

data.title: Sabra Health Care: A Compelling REIT Opportunity As SHOP Unlocks Hidden Value
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA) has been recovering well in recent years after being hit significantly during the pandemic, although I believe there's still plenty of potential left here, while the company pays an industry-leading yield and comes at some of the most attractive valuation ratios, offering a solid way to gain exposure to the significant potential of the skilled nursing, senior housing and behavioural health facilities across the US and Canada.

Over the first 9 months of 2025, SBRA delivered a decent 3.7% increase in Normalized AFFO per share, reaching $92.15 million during Q3 and $272.05 million over 9M'25, all while the company is investing record amounts that I'll mention below and has a strong pipeline going into 2026.

Regarding the guidance, Sabra sees a solid AFFO of about $1.455 to $1.465 per share and a normalized one that's $0.04 higher, meaning about $358.72 million in AFFO in 2025 or $368.55 million in Normalized AFFO at midpoint given the 245.7 million shares outstanding they use for the guidance. On a $4.7 billion market cap, that would place them at a P/NAFFO of about 12.75, which is not bad at this point.

Similar to peers and other companies across the broader healthcare industry, Sabra is advancing its transition to SHOP (reporting a 15.9% cash NOI growth for SHOP assets), opting for operating their own properties instead of relying on tenants, which allows them to capture the potential occupancy improvements, as I'll mention below.

Right now, SHOP reportedly stands at about 26% of the company's portfolio, growing "more quickly than anticipated," with the SBRA announcing a new target of 40% (vs. prior 30%) while also estimating that they'll exceed their $500 million upper end of the investment guidance, taking advantage of the weakness in this environment, which is very significant for a ~$4.7 billion market cap, with the management noting the following during their Q3 Earnings Call:

We will exceed the high end of our investment targets. Originally, our investment target was $400 million to $500 million. We will exceed the $500 million. In addition to that, the pipeline continues to be robust, and we'll be working on deals diligently, obviously, through the end of the year, which will allow us to get 2026 off to a much stronger start and should bode well for volume next year.

They also mention that the vast majority of these projects were closed in the second half of the year, meaning that their impact on 2025 will not be as visible as 2026, while their project pipeline is 90% to 95% SHOP, which has seen strong pricing power while the occupancy remained very solid.

Financially, based on SBRA's latest report, we see an overall solid position for a REIT, with a decent amount of cash on hand now and significantly more assets than liabilities. Again, it's important to note that especially when talking about a REIT playing in an industry with so much long-term potential, the real value of these real estate assets can be significantly higher, while their value is not only depreciated, but also recorded at the price of acquisition, creating a very significant gap between the balance sheet value and their real market value.

The company has an okay debt maturity schedule, with nothing to worry about until 2027, which can buy them a bit of breathing room before having to refinance at the currently high rates. Meanwhile, back in Q2, they closed an unsecured term loan for $500 million due in 2030, which they effectively fixed at a 4.64% rate thanks to interest rate swaps, with proceeds being used to repay their 2026 notes with a 5.125% rate.

The company is scheduled to report their Q4 earnings on February 25, with similar Normalized FFO estimates as last quarter and a higher revenue of $201.97 million. As this is the final report for the year, I believe it carries even more importance, as the company is likely to announce their latest guidance for 2026, which can lead to more volatility as the market could more accurately refine its expectations for the year.

Dividends

Regarding the dividends, SBRA currently pays a 6.38% yield, which was maintained at $1.20 following the ~33% cut during the pandemic - for obvious reasons. Still, based on the Normalized AFFO, we're talking about an almost exactly 80% payout ratio, which leaves some room for improvements once they see things stabilizing enough, and I wish analysts will ask about their views regarding a potential increase soon, although this stagnating dividend is not uncommon for the industry, especially as they have to focus on SHOP now.

Risks & Opportunities

Right now, the environment is probably the most important factor to consider for a company like SBRA, as their industry has been affected significantly during the pandemic, which caused occupancy rates to drop and was followed by rate hikes that caused even more disruption in an already weakened environment, even causing bankruptcies in some cases.

With high long-term rates impacting the SOFR that companies generally borrow at and pressure on stock prices causing higher dividend yields, REITs are in a particularly bad environment, as they have to distribute most of what they make back to us. This means that companies like SBRA can be "squeezed" into a bad environment with limited growth, as their cap rates are rarely able to beat such elevated debt costs.

I believe it is important to note since REITs are generally getting a more and more negative view from the market, as the asset class is underperforming in the environment seen over the past half a decade, which is normal.

However, I also see this kind of environment as one of the best times to buy them, and I'll give Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) as an example since I own it with an average cost of ~$28.7 per share. This means I am not only up ~52% since 2022 when I started buying, but I also basically "locked in" a sustainable yield of cost of about 9.33%, plus the potential long-term increases, which is honestly more than I could've asked for in such a short amount of time from an asset with so much long-term potential.

Meanwhile, the market continues to see pressure from several other factors, including renewed worries about tariffs and the Greenland situation (despite the recent framework), worries about the Fed's independence, etc., which I believe can increase the risk of seeing a "higher-for-longer" rate environment - which is bad for REITs in general - as the market could adjust the reward it demands from the US (e.g. yield on 10-year notes).

These fears were recently confirmed, with significant North European investors reportedly considering a shift away from the US given the rising risks, with two Nordic pension funds (Sweden's Alecta and Denmark's AkademikerPension) selling or being in the process of selling their US treasuries, which can be just the beginning of a wider diversification effort that can punish US equities.

Meanwhile, the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB) warned that the rising national debt could soon grow faster than the US economy itself, which they say could trigger "six distinct types of fiscal crises," potentially putting even more pressure on an already uncertain environment.

Still, it's important to note that this is a long-term investment first and foremost, and the company's switch to SHOP can unlock significant value in the long term, allowing them to capitalize on a potential rise in occupancy while the investment would be relatively minimal, basically offering significant organic upside potential without requiring them to invest in new properties at the cost of higher operating risks.

I'll note again that this SHOP transition basically allows them to gain significantly more than the rent, which is basically the same no matter whether the occupancy is 75% or 100% today, while self-managing these can allow them to gain and fully enjoy a potential increase (or suffer from the decrease), which is likely given the industry's dynamics.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diverse range of peers including Omega Healthcare Investors, CareTrust REIT (CTRE), National Health Investors (NHI), LTC Properties (LTC), American Healthcare REIT (AHR) and Welltower (WELL), we see some of the most attractive valuation metrics (and average balance sheet ones) from basically every point of view combined with the best dividend among the group, which could suggest that SBRA represents an opportunity in what used to be an overall distressed industry but with significant long-term potential.

Risks Reiterated

As a REIT, SBRA remains significantly exposed to macro factors, meaning that higher rates for longer can continue to put pressure on their ability to expand through debt or equity, which can be tricky especially if tenants start reporting more issues because of it.

Meanwhile, although the SHOP exposure offers more potential, it also comes with more operating risks, as the company becomes susceptible to the risks associated with the downside in the industry, leading to more volatility that can even be a risk for the dividend, which I believe could lead to some reset in the market, as older shareholders that wanted more stability may not agree with the new model.

Valuation

For SBRA's DCF to equity valuation, I will start with the estimated Normalized AFFO of $368.55 million in 2025 as the base, followed by a 5% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to their SHOP focus, industry tailwinds potentially boosting occupancy rates, better pricing power and potential benefits as a result of refinancing at better rates, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a 10% discount rate to account for a bit more volatility than before, and 245.7 million shares outstanding based on their guidance for 2025.

This way, we get to an equity value of $5.77 billion, for an intrinsic value of $23.49 per share, which is well above the current $18.82 if they manage to execute this "fundamental reset" and benefit from SHOP.

Conclusion

I rate SBRA a Buy, with the stock still having plenty of room to develop following their focus on SHOP that unlocks the industry's significant long-term potential, standing to benefit from macro tailwinds. There are certainly risks - even more now given the new focus - and the market will likely pay very close attention to their performance, but the overall reward justifies it.

data.title: SFL Corporation: Juggling Debt, Struggling Dividend
data.description:
SFL Corporation (SFL) is a maritime shipper that offers a dividend over 9%. The yield is likely priced as such because of weaknesses in the balance sheet. The threat of further cuts looms, and investors should seek a better discount before buying.

Summary of Original Thesis

When I wrote about SFL last July, I described its then-yield of 11% as attractive but vulnerable to cuts.

A maritime shipper, SFL was exposed to macro cycles, and these had materialized over the years with falls and rises in its annual dividend.

On a forward-looking basis, SFL's problem was significant debt maturities in each approaching year. Annual free cash flow over the past decade was generally insufficient to repay this. With long-term leases on their shipping business as well, this made it difficult for them to raise prices and increase cash flow, putting them in a tight spot.

I therefore believed dividend cuts were likely unless they could liquidate some assets to reduce their debt burden. I couldn't see the reason for a Buy rating, in spite of the high yield.

Subsequent Updates and Financials

The first matter to assess is the dividend. My concerns about a potential cut proved to be well-founded, as the quarterly distributions were cut from $0.27 per share to $0.20.

During the Q2 earnings call, management elaborated on the Board's decision to lower it:

We guided in the first quarter that unless we saw a clear path onwards of the near term for the Hercules that we might have just looked at the distribution, call it, longer-term distribution capacity in the company. So it's clear that the rig is warm stacked.

I'll reiterate SFL's business model for a moment. While they are a maritime shipper, they run a diversified business in this industry, owning assets that service multiple types of freight. They write long-term leases on these assets, typically spanning several years.

This portfolio of assets includes two offshore rigs, and the 50% utilization in the slide above reflects the fact that Hercules has no active lease and isn't earning for SFL. It is just creating maintenance costs; note that Q3 revenue for energy assets is $24M against opex of $21M. It's a much tighter margin than the rest of the portfolio because of this. As of Q3, management was not planning to dispose of the asset and was still seeking out a new tenant.

In addition to the dividend cut, total assets have declined by about $250M for the year, driven by dispositions and impairments in the year. Two sales of $57M per vessel were announced in December as well.

Their balance of debt, however, hasn't declined as much, down from $2,839M at the start of the year to $2,760M in Q3, but the cash balance did at least grow from $155M to $278M in that period. The overall trend shows the company trying to boost its liquid position to navigate near-term debt maturities.

Overall, SFL didn't experience serious problems, but the financials reflect a company that feels the weight of its debt.

Outlook and Valuation

At $8.36 per share, SFL offers a 9.57% dividend yield. This is also a slight premium to tangible book value per share, which was $7.47 in Q3. Given the $813M in short-term debt in the Q3 balance sheet, these are both figures that will be stress-tested.

Given the shrinking portfolio, the lack of any announcements for takers on a Hercules lease, and the fact that most of the upcoming debt needs to be repaid or refinanced, I see more room for a dividend cut to manage what is still a tight cash flow situation, as well as reduced TBV per share as assets are trimmed.

The yield is high but may not last long, and a discount to TBV is needed to protect one's principal in case the debt burden leads to some kind of restructuring or liquidation. Again, as their leases are long-term, pretty much the only near-term levers they can pull are dividend cuts or asset sales.

I maintain that SFL is only a Buy if the share price falls below $5 per share (provided such a drop accompanies similar financials) in order to create a basic margin of safety. SFL is not a stock to be purchased lightly.

Looking at the history of TBV per share, it's clear how much their capitalization lends itself to TBV per share erosion. $5 per share gives space for more contraction of the balance sheet as they rotate assets and debt obligations. It also gives a 16% dividend yield, giving something of a risk premium to shareholders.

Q4 2025 results have not been announced yet, but they should be released in about a month. Attention will need to be on the balance sheet and plans for the debt obligations.

Conclusion

Updates in the two quarters that followed were somewhat small, but they were very informative. They confirmed my view that SFL's capitalization makes a yield over 9% less and less likely.

To the extent that such concerns have materialized, SFL is down 21% over the past year. The yield is some form of compensation for this risk, but that's why this isn't a case of undervaluation. I maintain that SFL is a Hold with this assessment.

data.title: Tesla: Robotaxi Storm Clouds To Rain On Parade
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has soared to all-time highs recently due to the excitement over the push towards robots, led by robotaxis. CEO Elon Musk has long promised the robotaxi launch is just around the corner, while a prominent competitor is now full speed ahead on robotaxis. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock, with the current valuation pricing in a robotaxi success that isn't clearly visible with the persistent storm clouds likely to rain on the recent parade.

Smoke and Mirrors

Tesla investors have eagerly been awaiting the day when Elon Musk would announce the company has officially launched a robotaxi service. From there, investors and analysts could run the financials similar to ARK Invest and calculate a massive opportunity ahead.

The problem is that Tesla always ends up with a hiccup in the plan for launching unsupervised robotics. Just the fact the market has to distinguish between supervised and unsupervised vehicles is a big part of the problem.

On January 22, investors thought the day had finally come. On Twitter/X, Elon Musk confirmed Tesla was now driving robotaxis around Austin, TX, without a safety monitor. The positive momentum did not last.

Within hours, the market quickly figured out Tesla had moved the safety monitor to a chase vehicle. The EV company has apparently taken the safety monitor out of the robotaxi, but the news made it very unclear if the car is truly driving fully autonomous.

In fact, the Tesla robotaxi account on Twitter/X possibly misleads the market by saving the vehicles we're driving without a safety monitor. At least, Elon Musk stated the monitors were just removed from the car.

Prior to 2025, Elon Musk had made a long list of self-driving vehicle promises going back a decade, with Deepwater Asset Management detailing this long list as follows:

In 2025, Elon Musk ramped up the expectations with the official launch of the robotaxi operation in June following the Cybercab event back in October 2024. The only catch is that Tesla had to launch the business with a safety monitor in Austin and a regular ridesharing business in San Francisco.

The market wasn't worried due to constant promises the official service launch was right around the corner. Elon Musk even proclaimed on the Q2'25 earnings call in July that half the U.S. would have a robotaxi service by the end of 2025.

Musk again scaled back on the Q3'25 earnings call in late October. The CEO claimed Tesla would have robotaxi services in 8 to 10 metro areas by the end of 2025.

Alas, Tesla has started 2026 with more of the same. The company has started the year with more smoke and mirrors and more clear signs the service is far behind the competition.

The difference in the investment from years ago is that Tesla still has the ability to be the leader. The company could launch any day and quickly ramp up robotaxi services due to having manufacturing plants.

The stock is different now because Tesla no longer has a first-mover advantage. Waymo, owned by Alphabet (GOOG), already averages 450,000 robotaxi rides per week, offering services in 6 markets with plans to end 2026 with 1 million rides per week and services in 20 cities.

Every day Tesla is delayed, Waymo becomes more and more the verb for taking a robotaxi, just like googling is a term for an internet search.

The other big issue is that even Waymo is taking upwards of a year from starting testing in a new city to when the company actually launches unsupervised robotaxi services. The company just launched in Miami after announcing plans in late 2024 to start testing in early 2025.

Tesla isn't currently testing services in any other city and would need to provide regulators with testing data before an approval likely occurs. The quick ramps predicted by Elon Musk don't appear possible when even the robotaxi leader isn't accomplishing such moves.

Weak Results Require Robotaxi Success

Even if investors didn't always believe those promises, the market has bought into the most recent ones. For Q3, Tesla reported sales only rose 11% due to weak EV demand, and profits fell by 31% to an EPS of only $0.50.

The business is struggling, and new EV competition is heating up with Rivian Automotive (RIVN) launching the price-competitive R2 this year and Lucid Group (LCID) still in the game with increased production and the launch of a midsize crossover in 2026. Globally, the Chinese EV companies like BYD (BYDDF)(BYDDY) remain huge threats to Tesla, so the company isn't guaranteed to grow EV sales outside of a successful robotaxi.

Tesla will report Q4'25 results on January 28. The market isn't expecting anything better for the December quarter, with Q4 deliveries already reported to have fallen by 16% to 418K due in large part to the loss of tax credits.

  • EPS of $0.45, down 38% YoY
  • Revenue of $24.75 billion, down 3.7% YoY

The ARK Invest robotaxi opportunity probably has some realistic aspects to the market overall. The issue is that Tesla is no longer a first mover likely to capture the majority of the market, with Waymo already entrenched with 20 million rides completed and the likes of Zoox, owned by Amazon (AMZN), offering services in Las Vegas. On a global basis, Tesla already faces at least 5 robotaxi providers offering services already.

The market will be far more split up, though potentially enormous. ARK Invest outlined the potential need for 600k robotaxis in Austin alone, with larger cities reaching the millions, creating a $34 trillion enterprise value for the AV technology providers using a $1.8 trillion EBIT number and a 19x multiple.

The numbers are enormous, but Tesla has to actually launch services. For now, the stock trades at enormous valuations, such as over 206x forward EPS targets.

Tesla is currently worth $1.5 trillion. The stock could have some upside potential on any signs the company has finally unlocked the robotaxi opportunity and will scale faster than the competition. Otherwise, the valuation is just unsustainable based on recent results.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Tesla shows no signs of actually executing on the robotaxi opportunity. The stock shouldn't trade based on this premium, with Tesla and Elon Musk great at building new untapped markets where creativity and drive are more important than execution.

Investors should continue to use the current elevated stock price to sell Tesla on the ongoing robotaxi farce.
